pages.text: Compressive response of pervious concretes proportioned for desired porosities Omkar Deo a, Narayanan Neithalath a,b,⇑ a Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Clarkson University, Potsdam, NY 13699, USA b School of Sustainable Engineering and the Built Environment, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ 85287, USA article info Article history: Received 15 January 2011 Received in revised form 14 April 2011 Accepted 19 April 2011 Available online 23 May 2011 Keywords: Pervious concrete Pore structure Proportioning Porosity Critical pore size Compressive behavior Compressive energy abstract An understanding of the mechanical response of pervious concretes as a function of material design and pore structure parameters is essential for effective utilization of this material. A methodology to propor- tion pervious concrete mixtures of desired porosity using high or low cement paste contents is provided in this paper along with the compressive response of these mixtures. The compressive stress–strain rela- tionships demonstrate: (i) a consistent trend of decreasing peak stresses and strains at peak stress with increasing porosity, (ii) a reduction in strains at peak stresses with reducing paste contents, and (iii) a rapid drop in the post-peak response with reducing porosity. The compressive energy absorbed by the pervious concrete specimens is found to scale linearly with compressive strength and can be related to the porosity and critical pore sizes in the material. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The use of pervious concrete (or Enhanced Porosity Concrete) for sustainable construction activities continues to rise due to its several environmental beneﬁts. Most important among these beneﬁts is the reduction of storm water runoff and recharging of groundwater when used as a paving material for parking lots, low volume pavements, and sidewalks [1]. The ability of the pervi- ous concrete material structure to allow water to percolate into the underlying strata depends on its porosity, which is one of its most important pore structure features. Porosity (contributed by the large sized open pores) of these concretes typically ranges from 15% to 30%, and the characteristic pore sizes range from 2 mm to 8 mm depending on the type of aggregates and the method of compaction [1,2]. The porosity and other pore structure features play a signiﬁcant role in the functional and structural performance of pervious concretes [3–5]. The fact that pervious concretes are typically used for parking lots and low trafﬁc volume pavements has resulted in relatively less attention being focused on the resistance of the material to mechan- ical loads. Of late, there is renewed interest in the use of pervious concretes as surface courses for pavements (for tire–pavement interaction noise abatement) and other moderate to heavy load applications. While a few studies have reported the compressive and ﬂexural strengths of pervious concretes as a function of water-to-cement ratio, aggregate-to-cement ratio, and aggregate sizes [6–9], there is scant information on the dependence of the mechanical performance on the features of the material structure. A recent study [5], has provided details on the inﬂuence of pore volume, sizes, pore spacing, and pore speciﬁc surface area on the compressive stress–strain relationships of low to moderate com- pressive strength (8–12 MPa) pervious concretes. The changes in aggregate sizes provided the requi

4.75 mm sieve, retained on 2.36 mm sieve), (ii) #4 or 4.75 mm (passing 9.5 mm sieve, retained on 4.75 mm sieve), (iii) 3/8 00 or 9.5 mm (passing 12.5 mm sieve, re- tained on 9.5 mm sieve), and (iv) 1/2 00 or 12.5 mm (passing 25 mm sieve, retained on 12.5 mm sieve). Previous studies [10,11] have reported differences in pore struc- ture features such as pore sizes, connectivity, and dispersion at similar or compara- ble pore volume fractions when these aggregate sizes were used to proportion pervious concrete mixtures. The mixture proportioning procedure adopted in this study is explained in detail in a later section. 2.2. Test methods The test methods employed in this study include the determination of: (i) vol- umetric porosity, (ii) critical pore sizes using image analysis on planar sections of pervious concrete specimens, and (iii) stress–strain response of pervious concrete cylindrical specimens subjected to uniaxial compression. 2.2.1. Determination of volumetric porosity Volumetric porosity ( / v) of the pervious concrete specimens was measured using a procedure described in a previous study [3,11]. Cylindrical specimens (95 mm diameter /C2150 mm long) were submerged in water for 24 h to saturate the pores in the paste matrix. After removing them from water, the sides of the specimens were sealed with latex membrane. The specimens were placed on steel plates and the edges were sealed. Water was added from the top of the specimen, and the mass of water required to ﬁll the voids was converted into an equivalent accessible pore volume. This procedure was repeated on three specimens belonging to a particular mixture. 2.2.2. Determination of critical pore size A critical pore size ( d crit) that is expected to inﬂuence the compressive response was determined for all the pervious concrete mixtures using image analysis proce- dures on planar sections. While several methods have been developed to determine the characteristic pore sizes in pervious concrete, a granulometric opening distribu- tion function has been favored towards the determination of a critical pore size that can be adequately related to the transport characteristics of the material [2,11,12]. Two-dimensional, thresholded binary images of pervious concrete sections were subjected to opening granulometry using a circular structuring element. The pore size corresponding to the ﬁrst local maximum in the granulometric density function is denoted as the critical pore size. More details on granulometric size distribution measurements in pervious concretes can be found in [2,11]. 2.2.3. Determination of stress–strain response The compressive stress–strain relationships for various pervious concrete spec- imens were determined using a 490 kN closed-loop universal testing machine oper- ating in displacement controlled mode. A strain rate of 100 le/s was used for all the compression tests. The platen-to-platen displacements were recorded to determine the strains since strain gauges could not be mounted on the specimen surface due to the highly porous and irregular nature of the specimen sides. The specimen ends were ground in a grinding machine and further polished with grits of varying sizes to ensure a smooth surface. Neoprene–rubber pads of 60 durometer hardness were used at the specimen ends for compression testing. 3. Porosity-based material design of pervious concretes The proportioning of conventional concretes is based primarily on achieving a speciﬁed compressive strength, i

of additional paste volume required to obtain the desired porosity will depend on the speciﬁc gravity and packing density of the aggregates. Therefore, it is essential to carry out trial mixtures in order to arrive at acceptable values of additional paste volume, aggregate volume, or a combination of both, along with the comp- active effort that helps to achieve the desired porosity. 3.3. Material design parameters and ﬁnal proportions For the high paste content mixtures, the material design was based on the reﬁned paste volume fraction ( vpaste = vp/C0min + DV) along with standard the ASTM C 1688 compaction method, for all the three design porosities using the four different aggregate sizes. For the low paste content mixtures, the paste volume frac- tion was supplemented by a DV of 0.02–0.03, and the remaining required volume was provided by additional amount of aggregates. The compaction level was increased to three layers and 25 blows of Proctor hammer per layer as stated earlier. The fresh porosities of the proportioned mixtures were determined using the methodol- ogy described in ASTM C 1688. To prepare the specimens in cylinders for strength determination, the compactive effort was ad- justed so that the fresh porosity (in 0.007 m 3 containers as per ASTM C 1688) and the hardened porosity (measured using the vol- umetric method in 100 mm diameter /C2200 mm long cylinders) would be comparable. It was found that for the high paste content mixtures, a compactive effort of ﬁve drops of Proctor hammer per layer for two layers in the cylindrical molds was sufﬁcient to bring the hardened state porosities close to the design porosity (and the fresh porosity), whereas for the low paste content mixtures, the compactive effort required to achieve the same was 8 blows per layer for three layers. Table 1 shows the mixture proportions for all the twelve high paste content mixtures and the seven low paste content mixtures proportioned as part of this study. The fresh porosities of all the mixtures determined in accordance with either the ASTM C 1688 procedure for high paste content mixtures or the modiﬁed proce- dure (with increased compaction effort) for low paste content mix- tures, and the hardened porosities are also provided in this table. It can be seen that the fresh and hardened porosities are comparable for the mixtures reported here. When the measured fresh porosities matched the design poros- ities, specimens were cast in 100 mm /C2200 mm cylindrical molds. The specimens were allowed to remain in the molds for 24 h. After curing for 28 days in a moist chamber (>98% RH), 12.5 mm thick slices were carefully removed from the top and bottom ends of the specimens using a diamond tipped saw. The specimen ends were ground in a grinding machine and polished in order to ensure smooth and plane surfaces for compressive testing. 4. Compressive response of pervious concretes 4.1. Stress–strain relationships for pervious concretes in compression Numerous studies in the past have evaluated the stress–strain relationships for conventional concretes made using a wide variety of mixture design parameters and material inclusions [17–19].A recent study by the authors [5] has provided information on the general stress–strain behavior of pervious concretes, and devel- oped models to predict the stress–strain response, and the rela- tionships between the parameters of the stress–strain response and the pore structure. Fig. 4a–d depict representative stress

differences in the pre-peak responses of mixtures made using the same aggregate size but different design porosities diminish with a reduction in the aggregate size, i.e., the slopes of the ascending portions of the curves are similar for smaller aggregate size mix- tures irrespective of the porosity. This can be attributed to the fact that the pore structure of the specimens made using smaller aggre- gates is much more homogeneous than that of specimens made using larger aggregates. The inﬂuence of the dispersion of pores and their connectivity on the stress–strain response of pervious concrete mixtures made with different aggregate sizes has been explained earlier [5]. The specimens made using larger aggregates (100% 1/2 00) show lower peak strengths than the specimens having similar porosities made using smaller aggregates, especially at higher values of porosity. This is because of the very large size and connectivity of the pores in this mixture, and can be explained based on the ﬂaw size dependence of strength for quasi-brittle materials [20,21]. The stress–strain curves shown here also indicate that the spec- imens with lower porosity (and higher peak stresses) demonstrate a more brittle behavior (shown by a sudden drop in post-peak stress levels). Though this is consistent with results reported for conventional concretes where a higher strength implies increased brittleness, it is also expected to be contributed in part by the nature of testing. The platen-to-platen displacement was used to determine the strains because the irregular nature of pervious con- crete surface created difﬁculties in attaching strain gauges on the specimen surface. Usually, a high degree of sensitivity is not real- ized when the platen-to-platen displacement is used to character- ize the post-peak behavior, especially immediately after the peak stress is reached. However the stress–strain curves provide an ade- quate means to compare the compressive behavior of pervious concretes proportioned for different porosities and made using dif- ferent aggregate sizes. Fig. 5 a and b shows representative stress– strain relationships for the low paste content pervious concrete mixtures proportioned using 3/8 00 and #4 aggregates. The inﬂuence of porosity on peak stresses, and on the post-peak response is sim- ilar to those discussed earlier. A signiﬁcant difference in behavior between the high paste con- tent and low paste content mixtures relates to the observed strains at the peak stress. Fig. 6 a shows the relationship between the strain at peak stress and the corresponding peak stress for both the high paste and low paste content mixtures used in this study. The strain at peak stress is found to increase with an increase in the peak stress, which is in line with the observations reported for nor- mal concretes [22–24]. This relationship is dependent on the ef- fects of several factors such as the shape, size and other physical characteristics of the concrete, testing methods adopted, and the rate of loading [25]. In the case of pervious concretes, the highly (a) (b) (c) (d) 0 0.003 0.006 0.009 0.012 0.015 Strain 0 5 10 15 20 25 Stress, MPa 100% 1/2" φd = 0.19 (0.164) φd = 0.22 (0.245) φd = 0.27 (0.261) High Paste Content Strain 0 5 10 15 20 25 Stress, MPa 100% 3/8" φd = 0.19 (0.195) φd = 0.22 (0.238) φd = 0.27 (0.246) High Paste Content Strain 0 5 10 15 20 25 Stress, MPa 100% #4 φd = 0.19 (0.169) φd = 0.22 (0.223) φd = 0.27 (0.256) High Paste Content Strain

random nature of the pores and their dispersion leads to a further variability in this relationship. Hence, a very high correlation is of- ten not observed; however, this relationship plays an important role in the analytical modeling of stress–strain relationship [22]. The strains at peak stresses along with the ultimate material strength and a few parameters that are functions of the pore struc- ture features have been used previously to model the pre-peak and post-peak stress–strain responses of a few pervious concrete mix- tures in compression [5]. The strains at peak stresses are essentially lower for the mix- tures having low paste contents as compared to those with high paste contents. Since the elastic modulus of cement paste is typi- cally lower than that of the aggregates, it is expected that mixtures with a higher paste volume fraction will exhibit higher strain levels for the same ultimate stress. The differences in strains at peak stress between the high and low paste content mixtures decrease with increasing peak stress. Fig. 6b shows the relationship between the strains at peak stress and the porosity for the pervious concrete specimens made using both high and low paste contents. It can be seen that with an increase in the porosity of the specimens, there is a substantial reduction in the strains at peak stress. Higher porosity results in the specimens failing early (inverse dependence of strength on porosity); thus the corresponding strains are also low. 4.2. Relative inﬂuence of porosity and paste volume fraction on compressive strength Fig. 7 a shows the relationship between compressive strengths and hardened porosities for pervious concrete mixtures having both high and low paste contents. The error bars represent one standard deviation of compressive strength and porosity from their mean values. A 10% increase in porosity approximately halves the compressive strength of pervious concrete mixtures studied, irre- spective of the paste content and the compaction procedure. It is also seen that the compressive strength–porosity relationships of both the high and low paste content mixtures can be adequately expressed using a single exponential equation, even though indi- vidual expressions for both these cases lead to slightly improved correlation coefﬁcients. The responses of mixtures having three different design porosities (19%, 22%, and 27%) are shown for each of the chosen aggregate sizes. The error bars correspond to one standard deviation o

Fig. 7 b shows the relationship between the compressive strength and the paste volume fraction for pervious concrete mix- tures made using both high and low paste contents. Two distinct and essentially parallel relationships – one corresponding to the high paste content mixtures and the other corresponding to low paste content mixtures – are observed. The paste volumes for the low paste content mixtures are typically about 40% lower than those of high paste content mixtures for the same porosity, but the strength reduction is only of the order of 10–20%. This is con- tributed by the effect of increased amounts of aggregates and com- paction effort provided for the low paste content mixtures. This points to the fact that the paste volume fraction (and hence the ce- ment content) has only a relatively lesser inﬂuence on compressive strength when compared to the effect of porosity. In order to ex- plain the inﬂuence of paste volume fraction on compressive strength, other parameters such as the aggregate volume fraction and the compaction effort needs to be considered, whereas poros- ity is known to be the most important pore structure feature inﬂu- encing the mechanical response of porous materials. An analysis of variance (ANOVA) was carried out on a multiple regression model relating compressive strength to the pore and paste volume frac- tions, and it was found that the pore volume fraction (porosity) is the only signiﬁcant model term ( p-value < 0.05) as well as the term that showed the highest sensitivity towards the compressive strength. While only the inﬂuence of porosity and paste volume fraction on compressive strength has been considered in this study to keep the discussions succinct, it has been shown that pore sizes and three-dimensional pore connectivity also play a role in dictat- ing the compressive strength of pervious concretes, albeit to a les- ser degree than the porosity [5]. 4.3. Compressive energy absorption in pervious concrete mixtures The energy absorbed by the specimens under a compressive load can be quantiﬁed by measuring the area under the load–dis- placement curve. The greater the area under the load–displace- ment curve, the greater is the absorbed energy. For conventional and ﬁber reinforced concretes, energy absorption is quantiﬁed in terms of toughness index, which is the ratio of the area under load–displacement curve until a certain strain, e0 (e0 > epeak-stress) to the area under the curve up to the peak stress. Toughness index relates to the increase (or decrease) in energy absorption capacity of the concretes in comparison with a control concrete. Many studies in the past have explained the behavior of conventional and ﬁber reinforced concretes using the toughness criteria [26– 28]. In this study, compressive energy absorption values are evalu- ated in order to provide an estimate of the energy absorption capa- bilities of pervious concretes proportioned using different porosities, paste volumes, and aggregate sizes. The area under the load–displacement curve up to three times the displacement at peak load was used to compute the compressive energy absorp- tion [29–31]. This strain level corresponds to a stage where approximately 10–25% of peak strength remains in the post-peak region [31,32], which is found to be true for the pervious concrete specimens evaluated. Fig. 8 a shows the relationship between the solid volume frac- tion (1 /C0 /hardened) and the compressive energy for all the pervious conc

concrete mixtures is shown in Fig. 9 . An increase in porosity is found to reduce the compressive energy absorption of the pervious concrete mixtures, the reasons for which were explained earlier. For a certain design porosity, an increase in critical pore size (due to the increase in aggregate size [10,11]) also results in a reduction in compressive energy. While material properties like elastic modulus typically depend only on the volume fraction of the pore phase in many porous materials (as has been expressed using approaches such as effective medium theories), the stress concentrating effects of the pores reduce the toughness or the en- ergy absorption capacity. The effect of stress concentration de- pends on the size and geometry of the defect [33,34]; the larger the pore, the higher the stress concentration, and lower the tough- ness. The number and spacing of the large pores, which depend on the total pore volume and critical pore sizes, thus dictate the en- ergy dissipation behavior of pervious concretes. The dependence of compressive energy on the pore structure features of pervious concretes can be inferred from this ﬁgure. The fact that compressive energy scales linearly with compressive strength pro- vides indications that the dominant pore structure features inﬂu- encing both these properties could be similar. 5. Conclusions This paper has presented results on the compressive behavior of pervious concretes proportioned to achieve desired laboratory porosities using a chosen set of materials and appropriate compac- tion methods. The following conclusions are derived from this re- search study: /C15Proportioning of pervious concrete mixtures were carried out in this study by considering the paste volumes required to obtain desired porosities based on the packing provided by a standard compaction method (ASTM C 1688). Modiﬁcations to this com- paction method were made in order to reduce the overall paste volume (and thus the cement content). Two sets of mixtures – high paste content, and low paste content – were proportioned for three different design porosities (19%, 22%, and 27%) using four different aggregate sizes (#8, #4, 3/8 00, and 1/2 00) to study their compressive behavior. The compaction energies imparted to ﬁeld pervious concretes is expected to lie in between those for the high and low paste content mixtures. The adequacy of this mixture proportioning methodology was conﬁrmed by the closeness in the values of the design and obtained (both fresh and hardened) porosities for both high and low paste con- tent mixtures. /C15The compressive response of the pervious concrete mixtures made with both high and low paste contents using different aggregate sizes showed several distinct trends. A consistent trend of decreasing peak strengths with increasing porosity was clearly observed, as expected. The dependence of strengths on aggregate sizes (and thus the pore sizes) was evident at higher porosities. The strains at peak stresses for the low paste content mixtures were found to be lower as compared to those for the mixtures made with high paste contents. Increasing porosity also lowered the strains at the peak stresses. /C15While a unique strength–porosity relationship of acceptable statistical signiﬁcance was observed irrespective of the total paste contents in the mixtures, two distinct trends were observed corresponding to the low and high paste contents Solid Volume fraction,(1 - φhardened) 0 200 400 600 800 1000 1200

when the paste content was related to the compressive strength. The effects of relative volumes of aggregates and the compaction effort would need to be considered when paste contents are related to compressive strength of pervious concretes. /C15A reduction in porosity (or increase in bulk solid volume frac- tion) of the pervious concrete mixtures were found to result in an increase in the compressive energy absorbed. Compressive energy values were found to be lower by approximately 25% for the low paste content mixtures as compared to the high paste content mixtures of similar strengths. A reduction in compres- sive energy was also observed when the critical pore sizes in the mixture were higher.
